title: Nursing Knowledge Challenge
seoDescription: Take a fun 80-question nursing knowledge quiz covering care basics, patient safety, communication, observation, and practical judgement.
pageTitle: Only 8% Pass This Nursing Knowledge Challenge. Can You?
summary: A fun staged nursing knowledge quiz about care basics, patient safety, observation, communication, and calm judgement.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Nursing Knowledge Challenge?
homepage.summary: Think you can stay calm, caring, and sharp through 10 nursing-style rounds?

footer.aboutText: This nursing knowledge test is a staged quiz inspired by the everyday thinking skills used in safe, respectful patient care: observation, communication, prioritising needs, privacy, hygiene, and careful recording. Nurses work across many settings and often combine practical tasks with calm judgement and teamwork. This challenge turns those broad ideas into an accessible self-test for entertainment and curiosity. It is not a nursing qualification, clinical assessment, medical advice, or official training exam.

landing.quickStartText: The first checks feel simple. The later rounds need sharper care judgement.

profiles.title: You handled the hardest care decisions.
profiles.copy: Excellent practical reasoning across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Sharp nursing-style judgement.
profiles.copy: You stayed accurate as the care scenarios became more demanding. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good practical score.
profiles.copy: You showed steady thinking across several care rounds. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep building the method.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward careful checks, clear communication, and calm priorities. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: First Check Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The next round asks for sharper observation and calm care choices.

questions.prompt: Which habit is most useful before giving routine care?
questions.explanation: A care plan helps the nurse understand what is needed before acting.

questions.prompt: A patient drinks 2 units of water in the morning and 3 units later. How many units is that?
questions.explanation: 2 plus 3 equals 5.

questions.prompt: Which item is usually used to measure body temperature?
questions.explanation: A thermometer is used to measure temperature.

questions.prompt: A patient says they feel dizzy when standing. What is the safest first response?
choices: Ask them to sit or stay steady
Ignore it because it may pass
questions.explanation: Dizziness can increase fall risk, so steadying or sitting first is safer.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 1, 2, 3, 4, ?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 1 each time.

questions.prompt: Which word best matches good nursing communication?
questions.explanation: Clear communication helps patients and care teams understand each other.

questions.prompt: A nurse washes hands before and after care. What is the main purpose?
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene helps reduce the spread of germs.

questions.prompt: Which choice shows patient-centred care?
choices: Use the same response for everyone
questions.explanation: Patient-centred care begins by listening to the person.

stageGroups.title: Care Area Focus
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice focus. Now the questions test careful reading and simple priorities.

questions.prompt: A note says: check pulse, record temperature, offer water. Which task is missing from this list: pulse, water?
questions.explanation: Recording temperature is in the note but missing from the shorter list.

questions.prompt: A patient has 4 tablets in the morning box and 4 in the evening box. How many tablets are in both boxes?
questions.explanation: 4 plus 4 equals 8.

questions.prompt: Which action best protects privacy?
choices: Discuss care in a private place
Leave notes open on a table
questions.explanation: Private discussions help protect personal information.

questions.prompt: If all red labels need checking and this label is red, what must be true?
questions.explanation: The rule says every red label needs checking.

questions.prompt: Which pattern comes next: clean, check, record, clean, check, ?
questions.explanation: The pattern repeats clean, check, record.

questions.prompt: A patient asks the same question twice. What is the most helpful response?
choices: Say the question is not important
questions.explanation: Repeating information calmly can support understanding.

questions.prompt: Which sign should be reported promptly?
choices: A new sudden change in breathing
questions.explanation: A sudden change in breathing may need prompt attention.

questions.prompt: Which number is largest?
questions.explanation: 0.6 is larger than the other numbers.

stageGroups.title: Care Basics
stageGroups.encouragement: You are building momentum. Next comes a round of observation and practical judgement.

questions.prompt: Why is a patient identity check important before care?
choices: It helps match care to the right person
questions.explanation: Identity checks help ensure care is given to the correct person.

questions.prompt: A pulse is counted for 30 seconds and the count is 38. What is the estimated count for 60 seconds?
questions.explanation: Doubling 38 gives 76.

questions.prompt: Which item is most connected with wound protection?
questions.explanation: A clean dressing can help protect a wound.

questions.prompt: If a patient is on a fluid limit, which action is most sensible?
questions.explanation: Fluid limits require careful recording of intake.

questions.prompt: Which sequence shows a safe basic order?
questions.explanation: Checking first, acting appropriately, and recording is a safer order.

questions.prompt: A patient refuses a non-urgent task. What should happen next?
choices: Listen, explain, and report if needed
questions.explanation: Respectful care listens to the person and communicates concerns.

questions.prompt: A reading is very different from the previous one. What is a wise first step?
choices: Check the reading again if safe
questions.explanation: Unexpected readings often need a careful recheck.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an observation rather than a judgement?
questions.explanation: Walking 10 steps is a specific observation.

stageGroups.title: Patient Safety
stageGroups.encouragement: Strong care thinking. The next stage adds more numbers and judgement.

questions.prompt: A patient has a yellow fall warning. What should that mainly prompt?
questions.explanation: A fall warning reminds the team to support safer movement.

questions.prompt: A nurse checks a room every 2 hours. How many checks happen in 8 hours?
questions.explanation: 8 divided by 2 equals 4 checks.

questions.prompt: Which choice is safest when instructions are unclear?
questions.explanation: Clarifying unclear instructions reduces risk.

questions.prompt: A chart shows breakfast, lunch, dinner. Which comes next in the daily pattern?
questions.explanation: After dinner, a bedtime snack may be the next listed intake item.

questions.prompt: Which detail matters most when recording pain?
choices: The person’s reported level and location
questions.explanation: Pain records should include what the person reports and where it is felt.

questions.prompt: All protected rooms need protective steps. Room 5 is an protected room. What follows?
questions.explanation: The rule applies to every protected room, including Room 5.

questions.prompt: A patient wants to walk but seems unsteady. What is the best response?
choices: Support safe movement or get help
questions.explanation: Safe support respects movement while reducing risk.

questions.prompt: Which pair best matches cause and effect?
choices: Wet floor and higher fall risk
questions.explanation: A wet floor can increase fall risk.

stageGroups.title: Medication Mindset
stageGroups.encouragement: Good progress. Now the quiz asks for careful checks without specialist drug knowledge.

questions.prompt: Why are medicine labels checked carefully?
choices: To match the right medicine with the right person
questions.explanation: Label checks help match medicine to the correct person and plan.

questions.prompt: A medicine is scheduled every 6 hours. How many times is it scheduled in 24 hours?
questions.explanation: 24 divided by 6 equals 4.

questions.prompt: Which information should match before medicine is given?
questions.explanation: Identity and medication records should match before giving medicine.

questions.prompt: If a patient reports a new rash after a medicine, what is the best action?
questions.explanation: A new rash may matter and should be reported according to process.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is safest?
questions.explanation: A safe medicine process includes checking before giving and recording after.

questions.prompt: A patient says a tablet looks different from usual. What should happen?
questions.explanation: A concern about a medicine should be checked before it is given.

questions.prompt: A medicine is scheduled at 14:00 and is checked 2 hours later. What time is the check?
questions.explanation: Two hours after 14:00 is 16:00.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the safest assumption?
choices: Similar names are always the same
Every tablet has the same effect
questions.explanation: Unclear orders should be checked before action.

stageGroups.title: Observation Round
stageGroups.encouragement: You are past the halfway point. Next comes more careful clinical reasoning.

questions.prompt: A patient is usually alert but is suddenly very confused. What matters most?
choices: It is a change from normal
questions.explanation: A sudden change from normal can be clinically important.

questions.prompt: A temperature rises from 37 to 39. How many degrees did it increase?
questions.explanation: 39 minus 37 equals 2.

questions.prompt: Which observation is most specific?
choices: The patient walked 20 units with support
questions.explanation: A distance and support level make the observation specific.

questions.prompt: If all urgent changes must be reported and this is an urgent change, what follows?
questions.explanation: The rule says urgent changes must be reported.

questions.prompt: Which pattern fits hourly checks?
questions.explanation: Hourly checks are one hour apart.

questions.prompt: A patient looks well but says something feels wrong. What is the best response?
choices: Dismiss it because appearance is enough
questions.explanation: Patient reports can be important even when outward signs seem normal.

questions.prompt: Which detail helps compare two readings fairly?
questions.explanation: Using the same method makes readings easier to compare.

questions.prompt: A fluid chart shows 200 ml, 150 ml, and 250 ml. What is the total?
questions.explanation: 200 plus 150 plus 250 equals 600 ml.

stageGroups.title: Team Communication
stageGroups.encouragement: Clear communication matters. The next round tests priorities under pressure.

questions.prompt: Which message is clearest?
choices: The patient has new chest pain and looks pale
questions.explanation: It describes the concern clearly and specifically.

questions.prompt: A care update should mainly help the next nurse do what?
questions.explanation: Care Update shares key information so care can continue safely.

questions.prompt: If two tasks are urgent but one affects breathing, which should be prioritised?
questions.explanation: Breathing concerns usually need immediate attention.

questions.prompt: Three patients need water: one needs 150 ml, one 200 ml, one 250 ml. How much is needed total?
questions.explanation: 150 plus 200 plus 250 equals 600 ml.

questions.prompt: Which order best supports safe care update?
questions.explanation: A safe care update identifies the person, the concern, and what needs doing.

questions.prompt: A family member asks for private information in a public area. What is the best response?
choices: Move to a suitable process or private setting
Ignore privacy because they asked nicely
questions.explanation: Privacy should be protected even when questions are reasonable.

questions.prompt: Which statement separates fact from interpretation?
choices: Fact: temperature is 39; interpretation: this may show illness
Fact: the nurse is worried; interpretation: the chart is blue
Fact: the room is large; interpretation: water is wet
Fact: everyone knows; interpretation: it is obvious
questions.explanation: The measured temperature is a fact; the meaning is interpreted.

questions.prompt: A patient needs checks every 15 minutes for one hour. How many checks happen after the first one at minute 0?
questions.explanation: At 15, 30, 45, and 60 minutes there are 4 further checks.

stageGroups.title: Pressure Decisions
stageGroups.encouragement: That was a strong round. Now the questions combine safety, evidence, and judgement.

questions.prompt: A patient has a new symptom and the care area is busy. What should guide the response?
questions.explanation: Care decisions should be guided by clinical priority.

questions.prompt: A chart says intake is 900 ml and output is 600 ml. What is the difference?
questions.explanation: 900 minus 600 equals 300 ml.

questions.prompt: All high-risk patients need extra safety checks. Patient A is high-risk. What must happen?
choices: Patient A needs extra safety checks
questions.explanation: The rule applies to Patient A.

questions.prompt: Which information is least useful for urgent care priority?
questions.explanation: A favourite programme does not help decide urgent clinical priority.

questions.prompt: A pattern of readings is 80, 84, 88, 92. What comes next?
questions.explanation: The readings increase by 4 each time.

questions.prompt: A patient asks for help, but another patient has an emergency. What is the best approach?
choices: Address the emergency and arrange support for the other need
Help only the person who asked first
questions.explanation: Emergency needs take priority, while other needs should still be managed.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion is safest from one unusual reading?
choices: It should be checked in context
It means all previous records are wrong
questions.explanation: One unusual reading should be considered with context and checking.

questions.prompt: A task takes 12 minutes. Three similar tasks take how long?
questions.explanation: 3 times 12 equals 36 minutes.

stageGroups.title: Clinical Reasoning Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent. One final round remains, and it rewards calm, careful thinking.

questions.prompt: A patient improves after a new care plan, but also started extra physiotherapy. What is the main issue?
questions.explanation: A second change can also explain improvement.

questions.prompt: Which result best shows a reliable trend?
questions.explanation: Repeated similar readings are stronger evidence of a trend.

questions.prompt: A patient walks 15 units on Day 1, 20 units on Day 2, and 25 units on Day 3. If the pattern continues, what is Day 4?
questions.explanation: The distance increases by 5 units each day.

questions.prompt: A nurse has 45 minutes. One task takes 15 minutes and another takes 20 minutes. How much time remains?
questions.explanation: 15 plus 20 is 35, and 45 minus 35 leaves 10 minutes.

questions.prompt: Which assumption is risky?
choices: A quiet patient may still need help
A normal reading should be recorded
No complaint always means no problem
questions.explanation: A person may have a problem even if they do not complain.

questions.prompt: If all documented care must be accurate and this note is not accurate, what follows?
choices: It does not meet the documentation standard
questions.explanation: An inaccurate note fails the accuracy standard.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is fairest?
choices: Same patient, same method, different times
Different patients, different methods, no times
A complete chart against a rumour
questions.explanation: Fair comparison needs similar method and clear timing.

questions.prompt: A patient has two risks: falling and dehydration. Which plan is strongest?
choices: Address both risks with clear steps
questions.explanation: A strong plan recognises both risks and sets actions.

stageGroups.title: Final Nursing Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: You reached the final round. These questions reward safe priorities and precise reasoning.

questions.prompt: A patient suddenly becomes short of breath while another asks for a blanket. What should happen first?
choices: Ask the patient to wait without checking
questions.explanation: A sudden breathing concern is the higher immediate priority.

questions.prompt: A care score uses alertness, mobility, and pain. A patient scores 2, 3, and 4. What is the total?
questions.explanation: 2 plus 3 plus 4 equals 9.

questions.prompt: Which statement best shows professional judgement?
choices: I will check the facts before deciding
I will use the fastest answer
I will assume my first thought is right
questions.explanation: Professional judgement uses facts and updates with evidence.

questions.prompt: A pattern uses add 2, then double. Starting at 3 gives 5, 10, 12, 24. What comes next?
questions.explanation: After doubling to 24, the next rule is add 2.

questions.prompt: A patient says pain is 8 out of 10, but smiles while speaking. What is the safest interpretation?
choices: Smiling proves there is no pain
questions.explanation: A person’s reported pain should be taken seriously.

questions.prompt: Only authorised staff may access a record. This person is not authorised. What must be true?
questions.explanation: The rule excludes people who are not authorised.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports a concern about deterioration?
choices: A clear change from usual condition
questions.explanation: Deterioration is suggested by a meaningful change from usual condition.

questions.prompt: A nurse must choose between two safe plans. One is faster but less comfortable, the other is slower but better tolerated. What matters most?
choices: Balancing safety, comfort, and the patient’s situation
Choosing the plan with more words
questions.explanation: Good care balances safety, comfort, and individual needs.
